fact: December 16 is exactly 5 days before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which means the sun is practically at its laziest point of the year!
In Antarctica, the sun never sets around this time and penguins are having their version of summer beach parties on ice!
Tree sap in cold places has typically turned into nature's antifreeze by this date, preventing trees from becoming giant popsicles.
Arctic foxes have usually grown their fluffiest winter coats by now and look like walking cotton balls with eyes.
The moon appears to hang in the sky longer during December nights in the Northern Hemisphere because Earth is tilted away from the sun.
Hibernating animals like bears have been snoozing for weeks by December 16, and their heart rates have slowed down to about 8 beats per minute - slower than a sleeping human!
December 16 marks the time when ice crystals in clouds start forming those perfect six-sided snowflakes in northern climates.
Evergreen trees are showing off right now in northern regions because they're the only plants still green while everything else looks brown and dormant.
In the Southern Hemisphere, December 16 falls during their hottest part of summer when some places hit temperatures that could cook an egg on the sidewalk.
Reindeer and caribou have special blood vessels in their noses that warm up freezing air before it hits their lungs - like having built-in heaters in their nostrils!
The sun sets so early in northern places like Alaska that by December 16, some towns only get about 3 hours of daylight - less time than a movie!
